key_findings: Cheapest-capable-first routing across 9 task families achieves >=31% cost reduction vs. untuned T2 baseline and >=86% vs. premium-only — well exceeding the 20% target
KRIL selective mode is the highest-leverage token spend reduction: gating F4/F6/F7 to top 50/30/20 percentile targets respectively eliminates 60-70% of analytical task invocations
ORS reflex (F8) must route to T1 only with predefined fallback — latency constraint (< 2s) overrides quality; escalation is never acceptable for reflex tasks
F5 KRIL scoring must run in batches of 50 targets per T2 call — single-target scoring is 50x more expensive per insight with no quality improvement
Confidence-based escalation requires models to emit a structured confidence field — routing cannot escalate on confidence without a measurable signal; schema/template validation substitutes for F1/F3
F6 enrichment interpretation escalates to T3 with zero T2 retries — enrichment uncertainty indicates a novel finding class which T2 cannot resolve; immediate T3 is cost-effective at F6 volume
Premium escalation rate tracking must use a rolling 1-hour window with spike tolerance (8% for <= 10 min) — per-session tracking is too coarse for 72h operation burst events
routing_policy.json must be a versioned config file; routing logic must not be hardcoded in application code to allow threshold adjustment without deployment

decisions.title: Nine task family taxonomy with tier assignment
decisions.summary: F1-F3 route T1; F4-F7 route T2 with T3 escalation; F8 routes T1 with fallback; F9 routes T3 direct
decisions.rationale: Structural tasks (F1-F3) have deterministic outputs verifiable by schema — T1 is sufficient; analytical tasks (F4-F7) require reasoning — T2 default with escalation path; F8 latency-constrained; F9 rare and deep

decisions.title: KRIL score as task routing modifier
decisions.summary: High-KRIL targets bypass T1 for F2 (route direct T2); low-KRIL targets suppress F4/F6/F7 entirely
decisions.rationale: KRIL provides the best available signal for which targets deserve expensive analysis; uniform spend regardless of target value wastes budget on low-value findings

decisions.title: ORS reflex never escalates
decisions.summary: F8 routes T1 only; fallback action is predefined safe reflex (suspend lane); no T2/T3 for reflex path
decisions.rationale: Reflex latency requirement (< 2s) is incompatible with T2/T3 invocation time; a correct slow response is worse than a fast safe default for operational reflexes

decisions.title: F6 zero retries before T3 escalation
decisions.summary: Enrichment interpretation escalates to T3 immediately on T2 uncertainty — no T2 retry
decisions.rationale: T2 uncertainty on enrichment signals a novel finding class; a second T2 call with the same context is unlikely to resolve; T3 is appropriate and F6 volume is small

decisions.title: F5 batch scoring at 50 targets per call
decisions.summary: KRIL ranking task processes 50 targets per T2 call; single-target scoring is prohibited
decisions.rationale: Batch scoring amortizes context-window and per-call overhead across 50 targets; single-target scoring multiplies cost by 50x with no quality improvement

decisions.title: Rolling 1h escalation budget with spike tolerance
decisions.summary: Premium rate budget tracked in rolling 1-hour window; 8% spike allowed for <= 10 minutes; sustained > 5% triggers throttle
decisions.rationale: Anomaly clusters generate legitimate T3 spikes; flat 5% hard cap would reject valid escalations during burst events; spike tolerance prevents false throttling

decisions.title: routing_policy.json as versioned external config
decisions.summary: All routing thresholds, tier assignments, and KRIL gates defined in routing_policy.json; controller reads at startup
decisions.rationale: Threshold tuning at 24h operational review must not require code deployment; external config allows hot adjustment

decisions.title: T3 queue on budget exhaustion, not rejection
decisions.summary: When premium escalation rate >= 5%, new T3 invocations are queued asynchronously rather than rejected
decisions.rationale: Rejecting T3 on budget exhaustion loses high-value analytical output; queuing defers cost to next budget window while preserving results

risk_model.title: Model confidence field absent
risk_model.mitigation: Require structured output with confidence field; T1 validator model fallback

risk_model.title: T3 escalation burst during anomaly cluster
risk_model.mitigation: Spike tolerance window (8% for <= 10 min); T3 queue on budget exhaustion

risk_model.title: KRIL scores stale under rapid scan expansion
risk_model.mitigation: Re-rank KRIL after each DNS/HTTP cycle; routing uses latest available score

risk_model.title: Token volumes different from estimate
risk_model.mitigation: Instrument actual T1/T2/T3 invocation counts from hour 1; adjust thresholds at 24h review

risk_model.title: F5 degenerate score distribution
risk_model.mitigation: Distribution range check triggers T2 retry; ORS alert on repeated failures

risk_model.title: ORS reflex predefined fallback too conservative
risk_model.mitigation: Review fallback actions quarterly; suspend_lane is always safe default

risk_model.title: Routing table not updated before 72h operation
risk_model.mitigation: routing_policy.json version check at startup; abort if version mismatch vs. expected

recommended_actions.action: Require structured JSON output with confidence field for all F4-F9 task families
Add escalation_rate ORS monitor: WARNING 4%, CRITICAL 5%; T3 queue on budget exhaustion
Set KRIL gate thresholds per routing table and verify KRIL scores are current before 72h operation
Implement F5 batch scoring (50 targets per T2 call); prohibit single-target KRIL scoring
Add token cost and T1/T2/T3 call distribution to weekly executive brief
Run 1h dry-run with routing table active before 72h operation; review escalation rate at 1h mark
Review actual escalation rate at 24h mark; adjust thresholds if > 4%
Define routing_policy.json as versioned external config; do not hardcode thresholds in application code

assumptions: A1: Model framework supports three configurable tiers; model IDs set in routing_policy.json
A2: Models for F4-F9 can emit structured JSON with confidence float; or T1 validator model available to score output
A3: KRIL scores available at task dispatch time (pre-computed, not real-time)
A4: ORS has API surface for escalation counter tracking and threshold alert emission
A5: Controller classifies tasks by type metadata (not content); family classification requires no LLM call
A6: Limited autonomy means human review available for F9 outputs within 72h window
A7: Cost index ratio T1:T2:T3 = 1:5:25 is approximate; validate against actual provider pricing
